title: Progressive Compression with Universally Quantized Diffusion Models

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Universally Quantized Diffusion Models (UQDM), a new diffusion-model formulation tailored for end-to-end progressive data compression. By replacing the Gaussian forward “posterior” channels with uniform noise channels and matching their first two moments to those of Gaussian diffusion, the method enables efficient channel simulation via universal quantization (UQ), thereby avoiding the exponential runtime of Gaussian relative entropy coding (REC). The authors provide theoretical connections to VDM in the continuous-time limit, and demonstrate competitive rate–distortion and rate–realism performance on CIFAR10 and ImageNet64 with a single progressive bitstream that supports both lossy and lossless compression.
strengths: Introduces a principled diffusion-model variant whose forward “posterior” is uniform, enabling exact and tractable channel simulation with universal quantization at each step.
Derives a reverse process as a density gθ convolved with a uniform kernel, aligning the coding distribution with the target UQ channel and grounding the bit-cost in the diffusion ELBO.
Provides a moment-matching construction and shows that the UQDM forward process converges to the Gaussian case in the continuous-time limit, offering theoretical reassurance and interpretability.
Employs learned reverse-process variance (per-coordinate), adapted from diffusion literature, and shows clear empirical benefits.

Validates on toy data and two natural image datasets (CIFAR10, ImageNet64), reporting both distortion (PSNR) and realism (FID) across bitrates and multiple progressive reconstruction strategies (denoise, ancestral, flow-based).
Includes comparisons with traditional codecs (JPEG, JPEG2000, BPG) and a progressive learned baseline (CTC), demonstrating strong results and progressive improvements over a wide bitrate range.
Reports that realized file sizes are within 3% of the theoretical NELBO, supporting the coding-theoretic claims operationally.
Clear exposition of the REC challenge and why Gaussian channel simulation is computationally intractable, motivating the switch to uniform channels.
Precise statement of the forward/backward processes, with intuitive moment matching and concise encoding/decoding pseudocode.
Helpful background on UQ and a clean connection between ELBO terms and progressive coding costs.
Achieves a practically deployable progressive codec with a single unconditional diffusion model that seamlessly supports lossy-to-lossless and can, in principle, approach perfect realism.
Bridges a salient gap between theory (progressive diffusion coding) and practice (tractable channel simulation and entropy coding), potentially influencing future generative compression systems.
weaknesses: The forward process is specified directly via conditionals (non-Markovian, DDIM-style) rather than derived from a single Markov kernel; while justified by moment matching and the CLT result, this introduces modeling choices that are not uniquely determined or theoretically optimal.

The learned reverse density class (logistic/Gaussian convolved with uniform) may be too restrictive; the gap to the hypothetical Gaussian diffusion performance remains, and the paper does not explore richer families (e.g., mixtures) that might close it.

The approach relies on shared randomness for zT and for UQ dithers across all steps; while standard in coding theory, practical synchronization/seed management and determinism implications are not discussed.

Evaluations are limited to small resolutions (CIFAR10, ImageNet64). No results on standard higher-resolution compression benchmarks (Kodak, CLIC, DIV2K) where perceptual codecs are typically measured, limiting conclusions about real-world applicability.

Runtime/latency and computational cost are not quantified (e.g., ms/image, steps vs. wall-clock), nor compared against existing progressive codecs (e.g., CTC) or traditional progressive modes (JPEG2000). This is crucial for compression practice.
Ablations on key design choices (e.g., learned variance, distributional choice for gθ, T sensitivity on real datasets) are sparse; swirl data ablations are informative but not definitive for images.

Some forward-process parameterizations (e.g., different expressions for Δ(t) and βt|t−1 in the main text vs. appendix) would benefit from a single, consolidated definition and a short derivation in the main paper for readability.

The practical details of discretizing p(zt−1|zt) for arithmetic coding per grid-offset u and managing high-dimensional tensors are largely delegated to the appendix/code; a brief complexity discussion in the main text would help.

Progressive learned codecs beyond CTC, such as DeepFGS/other scalable coding methods, are mentioned but not evaluated; even a qualitative comparison of progressive granularity and overhead would strengthen the empirical case.

Recent strong generative codecs (e.g., HFD) are non-progressive but relevant for the rate–realism frontier; acknowledging their operating points and trade-offs would better contextualize claims about realism potential.
reviews.questions:
1. Could you report measured LT values (and their variance across images) to substantiate that q(zT|x) ≈ p(zT) indeed makes the initial step cost negligible in practice?
2. How do encoding/decoding runtimes (and memory usage) scale with T and image size in your implementation, and how do they compare with CTC and JPEG2000 progressive modes on the same hardware?
3. For the entropy coding of kt, how exactly do you discretize p(zt−1|zt) per offset u (e.g., per-voxel independent bins vs. grouped channels), and do you model dependencies across spatial or channel dimensions in p(k|zt, u)?
4. Could you provide ablations on CIFAR10/ImageNet64 for learned reverse variance vs. fixed variance and for the choice of gθ (logistic vs. Gaussian vs. a lightweight mixture), quantifying both NELBO and RD/FID changes?
5. Have you experimented on standard higher-resolution benchmarks (e.g., Kodak, CLIC)? If not, what are the current limitations (compute, memory, training stability), and do you anticipate the T ≈ 4 setting to remain near-optimal at larger scales?
6. How sensitive are results to the choice of αt, σt schedules? Did learning the schedules bring any consistent gains, and if not, do you have a hypothesis for why linear schedules suffice here?
7. When targeting lossless completion, how close is the final codelength to PNG/FLIF or modern lossless learned compressors on your datasets, and does the learned p(x|z0) introduce any notable overhead?
reviews.assessment: This paper makes a clear, well-motivated, and technically sound contribution by enabling practical progressive diffusion-based compression via universal quantization. The modeling choices are sensible and well-justified, the coding interpretation is operational (with file sizes close to the ELBO), and the empirical results on CIFAR10 and ImageNet64 are promising, surpassing classic codecs and matching or exceeding a progressive learned baseline in many regimes. The primary limitations are scope and completeness of the experimental evaluation: results are restricted to low resolutions, runtime is not reported, and ablations on real image datasets are limited. Nonetheless, the core idea—replacing Gaussian channel simulation with UQ through a uniform “posterior” design—is compelling and likely to spur follow-up research that scales the approach, integrates faster samplers/distillation, and explores richer reverse distributions. On balance, I view this as a strong and timely step toward practical progressive generative compression and suitable for ICLR, contingent on clarifying runtime and adding a bit more empirical depth.
